palestinian.description: Long "ah" sound; also acts as a carrier for hamza.

lebanese.description: Long "ah" sound; often slightly fronted toward "æ" in Lebanese speech.

palestinian.description: Alif preceded by a glottal stop with a long "ah" sound — historically hamza + alif. Common in words like آدم (Adam) and القرآن (Quran).

lebanese.description: Glottal stop + long "ah".

palestinian.description: Like English "b".

lebanese.description: Like English "b".

palestinian.description: Like English "t" but unaspirated (no puff of air).

lebanese.description: Like English "t" but unaspirated.

palestinian.description: In Palestinian, usually merged with ت (/t/) in everyday speech, or with س (/s/) in loanwords from MSA. The classical /θ/ ("th" in "think") is rare colloquially.

lebanese.description: In Lebanese, usually merged with ت (/t/) in everyday speech, or with س (/s/) in MSA-influenced words.

palestinian.description: "Zh" sound, like the "s" in "measure". Some rural areas use /dʒ/ ("j" in "judge").

lebanese.description: "Zh" sound, like the "s" in "measure".

palestinian.description: Voiceless pharyngeal fricative — a "heavy", breathy "h" produced by tightening the throat. No English equivalent.
englishExamples: (no direct equivalent — like a strained whisper of "h")

lebanese.description: Voiceless pharyngeal fricative — a "heavy", breathy "h" from the throat. No English equivalent.
englishExamples: (no direct equivalent — like a strained whisper of "h")

palestinian.description: Like the "ch" in Scottish "loch" or German "Bach" — a raspy back-of-throat sound.

lebanese.description: Like the "ch" in Scottish "loch" or German "Bach".

palestinian.description: Like English "d".

lebanese.description: Like English "d".

palestinian.description: Usually merged with د (/d/) colloquially, or with ز (/z/) in MSA-influenced words. The classical /ð/ ("th" in "this") is rare colloquially.

lebanese.description: Usually merged with د (/d/) colloquially, or with ز (/z/) in MSA-influenced words.

palestinian.description: A flapped or trilled "r", like Spanish or Italian "r".

lebanese.description: A flapped or trilled "r", like Spanish or Italian "r".

palestinian.description: Like English "z".

lebanese.description: Like English "z".

palestinian.description: Like English "s".

lebanese.description: Like English "s".

palestinian.description: Like English "sh".

lebanese.description: Like English "sh".

palestinian.description: Emphatic "s" — pronounced with the back of the tongue raised, giving surrounding vowels a darker, deeper quality.
englishExamples: (no equivalent — "s" with a darker, hollower vowel)

lebanese.description: Emphatic "s" with the back of the tongue raised.
englishExamples: (no equivalent — "s" with a darker, hollower vowel)

palestinian.description: Emphatic "d" — pronounced with the back of the tongue raised; vowels around it sound darker.
englishExamples: (no equivalent — "d" with a darker, hollower vowel)

lebanese.description: Emphatic "d" with the back of the tongue raised.
englishExamples: (no equivalent — "d" with a darker, hollower vowel)

palestinian.description: Emphatic "t" — pronounced with the back of the tongue raised; surrounding vowels become darker.
englishExamples: (no equivalent — "t" with a darker, hollower vowel)

lebanese.description: Emphatic "t" with the back of the tongue raised.
englishExamples: (no equivalent — "t" with a darker, hollower vowel)

palestinian.description: Usually pronounced as an emphatic "z" colloquially. Classically /ðˤ/ (emphatic "th" in "this").

lebanese.description: Usually pronounced as an emphatic "z" colloquially.

palestinian.description: Voiced pharyngeal fricative — produced by constricting the throat as if starting to gag, then voicing. Has no English equivalent and is distinctive in Arabic.

lebanese.description: Voiced pharyngeal fricative — a deep throat sound with voicing.

palestinian.description: Like a French "r" in "Paris" — a voiced version of خ.

lebanese.description: Like a French "r" in "Paris" — a gargling sound.

palestinian.description: Not a letter — a horizontal extender used to stretch words for justification or emphasis. Has no sound and is typographic decoration only.

lebanese.description: Visual extender for justification; no sound.

palestinian.description: Like English "f".

lebanese.description: Like English "f".

palestinian.description: In urban Palestinian speech, pronounced as a glottal stop /ʔ/ (the catch in "uh-oh"). In rural Palestinian, often /k/ or even /g/. Classical /q/ is uvular (further back than /k/).

lebanese.description: In urban Lebanese, almost always pronounced as a glottal stop /ʔ/. Some communities (e.g. Druze) preserve classical /q/.

palestinian.description: Like English "k" but unaspirated.

lebanese.description: Like English "k" but unaspirated.

palestinian.description: Like English "l". Becomes "darker" (velarized /lˤ/) in the word "Allah" (الله).

lebanese.description: Like English "l". Becomes "darker" in the word "Allah".

palestinian.description: Like English "m".

lebanese.description: Like English "m".

palestinian.description: Like English "n".

lebanese.description: Like English "n".

palestinian.description: Like English "h" — pronounced even at the end of words.

lebanese.description: Like English "h"; can be lightly pronounced or omitted at word ends colloquially.

palestinian.description: As consonant: like English "w". As long vowel: like "oo" in "moon" (/uː/) or "o" in "go" (/oː/).

lebanese.description: Same range as Palestinian — consonantal "w" or long /uː/ / /oː/.

palestinian.description: As consonant: like English "y". As long vowel: like "ee" in "see" (/iː/) or "ay" in "say" (/eː/).

lebanese.description: Same range as Palestinian.

palestinian.description: Glottal stop — the "catch" between syllables in "uh-oh".

lebanese.description: Glottal stop — the "catch" sound.

palestinian.description: Hamza (glottal stop) carried on alif, usually at the start of a word with a short "a" or "u" sound.

lebanese.description: Glottal stop on alif at the start of words.

palestinian.description: Hamza on alif with a short "i" sound; appears at the start of words.

lebanese.description: Hamza on alif with a short "i" sound.

palestinian.description: Hamza carried on waaw — the waaw is just a chair, the sound is glottal stop.

lebanese.description: Hamza on waaw chair — glottal stop.

palestinian.description: Hamza carried on yaa — the yaa is a chair, the sound is glottal stop.

lebanese.description: Hamza on yaa chair — glottal stop.

palestinian.description: "Dotless yaa" — at word end, sounds like a long "ah". Functions as alif in pronunciation but is written as a yaa-shape.

lebanese.description: "Dotless yaa" — at word end, sounds like a long "ah".

palestinian.description: Feminine word ending. In standalone speech, pronounced as a short "a" or "e". When followed by another word in construct, becomes /t/.

lebanese.description: Feminine word ending, typically pronounced as a short "e" in Lebanese.

palestinian.description: "Connecting alif" — pronounced at the start of an utterance, but elided when joined to a preceding word. Used in Quranic spelling and in words like ٱللّٰه.

lebanese.description: "Connecting alif" — elides into the preceding word.

palestinian.description: Short "a" vowel placed above the preceding consonant.

lebanese.description: Short "a" vowel; in Lebanese often fronted toward "æ".

palestinian.description: Short "i" vowel placed below the preceding consonant.

lebanese.description: Short "i" vowel; in Lebanese often closer to "e".

palestinian.description: Short "u" vowel placed above the preceding consonant.

lebanese.description: Short "u" vowel; in Lebanese often closer to "o".

ْ.name: sukun
transliteration: none — marks the absence of a vowel

palestinian.description: Marks the absence of a vowel — the consonant is pronounced without a following vowel.

lebanese.description: Marks the absence of a vowel — the consonant is pronounced without a following vowel.

palestinian.description: Doubles the consonant — held twice as long. Like the difference between "midday" (held "d") and "Friday".

lebanese.description: Doubles the consonant — held twice as long.

palestinian.description: Tanwin: short "an" at the end of indefinite nouns. Mostly formal/MSA; rare in everyday colloquial.

lebanese.description: Tanwin: short "an" at end of indefinite nouns; mostly formal in colloquial speech.

palestinian.description: Tanwin: short "in" at end of indefinite nouns; rare in colloquial.

lebanese.description: Tanwin: short "in" at end of indefinite nouns; rare in colloquial.

palestinian.description: Tanwin: short "un" at end of indefinite nouns; rare in colloquial.

lebanese.description: Tanwin: short "un" at end of indefinite nouns; rare in colloquial.

palestinian.description: A wavy mark over alif (forming آ) indicating a glottal stop followed by a long /aː/. Rarely seen as a standalone combining mark — usually part of آ.
englishExamples: (combine with alef → آ as in آدم)

lebanese.description: Wavy mark indicating glottal stop + long /aː/, usually as part of آ.

palestinian.description: A combining hamza placed above its base letter — used to compose أ (alif+hamza-above) or ؤ / ئ in some Quranic spellings. Rarely typed as a standalone mark.

lebanese.description: Combining hamza above; rarely typed standalone.

palestinian.description: A combining hamza placed below its base letter — used to compose إ (alif + hamza-below). Rarely typed as a standalone mark.

lebanese.description: Combining hamza below; rarely typed standalone.

palestinian.description: A small alef written above the line; pronounced as a long "ah". Appears in fixed words like الله and هذا.

lebanese.description: Small alef above the line; pronounced as long "ah".
